records.prompt: Some suggest that we must assume logic as an unevidenced presupposition to ground all else we believe. However, children seem to learn logic inductively. They may first learn logic through their parent and teachers whom they have learned to trust through inductive experience. As they mature, they inductively test logic every day through their inductive experiences, rarely, if ever, encountering violations of logic. Is not this inductive grounding of logic more epistemically honest than leaping to the unevidenced presupposition logic is reliable?
records.heading: Presuppositional View of Logic gives the response its center of gravity.
reasons: The section has enough source structure and explanatory density for now.

records.prompt: I am going to push back against your synthesis by addressing each of your three points in favor of accepting logic as an unevidence presupposition. 1: Logical Framework — While logic does offer a framework for common deductive thought processes, it should not be grounded on an unevidenced presupposition. While inductive grounding of logic is sub-absolute (necessarily less than fully certain), the inductive density of logic we arrive at through our inductive experience is more than enough to give us ample confidence that logic is ontologically “solid” and will persist into the future. 2: Self-evident Logic: The high reliability of logic seen in our inductive experience may feel like a justification for its “self-evident” essence, but it is actually only our inductive experience that generates this intuition of its “self-evidence”, whatever that term may mean. 3: Circularity: You only find actual circularity in deduction. If your epistemology is grounded in induction, you are always leaving open the possibility that logic could fail, in which case you would simply revise your confidence in logic. There is no circularity in a degree of confidence in the reliability of logic that maps to the degree of inductive evidence for the reliability of logic. Would you not agree?
records.heading: Logical Framework and Inductive Grounding gives the response its center of gravity.
reasons: The section has enough source structure and explanatory density for now.

records.prompt: Allow me to respond to one line in your response. You said, “ We use logic to reason about evidence, so how can we justify its validity using logic itself? “ You cannot justify logic’s validity without reaching an inductive substrate. That inductive substrate is quite firm in the minds of most humans who employ (and thereby test) logic every day. That sub-absolute, though extremely high, justified degree of confidence in the reliability of logic is enough to justify the deductive use of logic in many domains. If logic were to ever fail, we would simply, at that point, lower our degree of confidence in logic as honest epistemic agents.
records.heading: Inductive substrate is the pressure point.
reasons: The section has enough source structure and explanatory density for now.
